narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and LSAT score of 31—both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among students in the dataset used for this prediction. The admissions model, which forecasts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, calculated a 91.7% probability that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. This high failure risk directly led to the denial, as the university prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 1, again well under the typical 5.7 average, reinforced concerns about consistent academic performance from early on. Your family's upper-middle income level also nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, as it deviated from patterns associated with stronger bar passage rates in the data. While other elements like your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of successful students, these top three academic and background factors dominated the model's reasoning. Compared to typical students who pass, your low deciles put you at a clear disadvantage, as higher rankings in those years are strong predictors of bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience—many from similar backgrounds overcome these hurdles—but the model weighs these metrics heavily based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your grades in any ongoing studies to improve your decile rankings, and seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. You might also explore bridge programs or schools with holistic reviews that factor in your unique story beyond these numbers. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile for future applications and build the skills needed to beat the odds on the bar.
